request.question: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?

title: Catfish Creek (Iowa)
Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River)
Rainwater Creek
Passage Creek
North Fork Flathead River
Black Creek (Florida)
Smith Creek (Virginia)
Shenandoah River
Los Baños Creek
Yager Creek
sentences: Catfish Creek is a 21.2 mi tributary of the upper Mississippi River in northeastern Iowa.
It rises southwest of Dubuque, just southeast of Peosta.
The Middle Fork rises west of Dubuque and flows under and then parallel to U.S. Route 20; the former Illinois Central Railroad, now the Chicago Central and Pacific Railroad has its mainline in it.
The remaining forks of the creek are all south of U.S. Route 52.
Swiss Valley Park, maintained by Dubuque County, is along the main fork of the creek, which is a designated trout stream through the park.
In recent years, people have been trying to stop the erosion in this relatively flat way out of the Gorge of the Upper Mississippi.
The North Fork of the creek has been proposed for a trail/park.
Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.
It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.
Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.
Rainwater Creek is a 3.9 mi tributary stream of Lake Fork Creek.
It flows into Lake Fork Creek downstream from the Lake Fork Reservoir Dam approximately four miles west of Quitman in western Wood County, Texas.
Its primary source is a spring, augmented by stormwater runoff.
It is named for the family of William Walton Rainwater, who was among the earliest permanent settlers of the area.
Passage Creek is a 38.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in Fort Valley, Virginia.

For most of its length it flows through a rural valley between the two spine-like ridges of Massanutten Mountain, then exits the valley by cutting a narrow gorge through the northeast end of the mountain.
The North Fork Flathead River (Ktunaxa: "kqaskanmituk" ) is a 153 mi river flowing through British Columbia, Canada, south into the U.S. state of Montana.
It is one of the three primary forks of the Flathead River, the main inflow of Flathead Lake and a tributary of the Columbia River via the Clark Fork River and the Pend Oreille River.
The river is sometimes considered the upper headwaters of the Flathead River, although the North Fork is its official name in the U.S.
Other naming conventions for the river include Flathead River - North Fork, North Fork of Flathead River, and North Fork of the Flathead River.
Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.
It is located in Clay County, Florida.
Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.
North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.
North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.
South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.
The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.
Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.
Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.
Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.
The Shenandoah River is a tributary of the Potomac River, 55.6 mi long with two forks approximately 100 mi long each, in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.

The principal tributary of the Potomac, the river and its tributaries drain the central and lower Shenandoah Valley and the Page Valley in the Appalachians on the west side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, in northwestern Virginia and the Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia.
Los Baños Creek or Los Banos Creek, originally El Arroyo de los Baños, is a tributary stream of the San Joaquin River.
Its source drains the slopes of the Diablo Range within the Central Valley of California, United States.
Los Baños Creek has its source at the confluence of North Fork Los Banos Creek and South Fork Los Banos Creek.
It flows northeast to the west edge of Los Banos and then north to Mud Slough 2.5 miles (4 km) upstream from its confluence with the San Joaquin River.
Yager Creek is a tributary stream of the Van Duzen River on the north coast of California in Humboldt County, California.
It has its source at the confluence of North Fork Yager Creek and Middle Fork Yager Creek.
Its mouth is at the confluence with the Van Duzen River just below the town of Carlotta.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?
hop1_docs: Smith Creek (Virginia) | Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.  Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.  Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.

Hayfork Creek | Hayfork Creek is a tributary of the South Fork Trinity River in Northern California in the United States.  At over 50 mi long, it is the river's longest tributary and is one of the southernmost streams in the Klamath Basin.  It winds through a generally steep and narrow course north then west through the forested Klamath Mountains, but also passes through the Hayfork and Hyampom Valleys, which comprise the primary agricultural regions of Trinity County.

North Fork Embarras River | The North Fork Embarras River is a 64.0 mi stream which originates in Edgar County, Illinois, and flows through Clark, Crawford and Jasper counties.  Its confluence with the Embarras River is located near the town of Ste. Marie in Jasper County.  It is a major tributary to the Embarras River.  The North Fork's main tributary is Panther Creek, located in Jasper County.

Black Creek (Florida) | Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.  It is located in Clay County, Florida.  Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.  North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.  North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.  South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.  The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.

North Fork Smith River (California) | The North Fork Smith River is 28 mi tributary of the Smith River that begins in the U.S. state of Oregon and ends in the U.S. state of California.  Arising near Chetco Peak in the Klamath Mountains, it flows generally south to meet the Middle Fork Smith River at Gasquet, California.  The combined streams form the Smith River.

Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River) | Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.  It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.  Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.

South Fork Crooked River | The South Fork Crooked River is a tributary, 76 mi long, of the Crooked River in the U.S. state of Oregon.  Starting southeast of Hampton in Deschutes County, the South Fork flows east from near Ram Lake (dry).  It passes under U.S. Route 20 at Hampton, then continues east and north for about 6 mi before entering Crook County.  Shortly thereafter, east of Hampton Buttes, it receives Buck Creek from the right and then passes through Logan Reservoir.  Flowing north, it receives Sand Hollow Creek from the left, then Twelvemile Creek from the right before crossing under Oregon Route 380 (Southeast Paulina Highway).  Just north of the highway, the river joins Beaver Creek to form the Crooked River 125 mi from its confluence with the Deschutes River.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Smith Creek — [1]: "35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River"
- Smith Creek location — [1]: "Shenandoah Valley of Virginia."
- North Fork Shenandoah River — [6]: "North Fork Shenandoah River" (in context of a tributary)
Ambiguities:
- "Smith Creek" could be a common name for multiple streams (passages only contain "Smith Creek (Virginia)"); no alternate Smith Creek pages shown.
- The target river is referred to as "North Fork of the Shenandoah River" and "North Fork Shenandoah River" (variant phrasing in passages).
Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state which US state(s) the Shenandoah River passes through (the relation "Shenandoah River — passes through which US states?" is unresolved).
Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page: "Shenandoah River"
- Fetch page: "North Fork Shenandoah River"
- Search query: "Which US states does the Shenandoah River pass through?"
- Search query: "Shenandoah River course states Virginia West Virginia"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: Shenandoah River states Virginia West Virginia
hop2_docs: Shenandoah River | The Shenandoah River is a tributary of the Potomac River, 55.6 mi long with two forks approximately 100 mi long each, in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.  The principal tributary of the Potomac, the river and its tributaries drain the central and lower Shenandoah Valley and the Page Valley in the Appalachians on the west side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, in northwestern Virginia and the Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia.

Little Dry River | The Little Dry River is a 10.9 mi tributary of the North Fork Shenandoah River in the U.S. state of Virginia.  It rises in Rockingham County just east of the Virginia-West Virginia border near the crest of Shenandoah Mountain and flows east, joining the North Fork just west of the village of Fulks Run.

U.S. Route 33 in West Virginia | In the U.S. state of West Virginia, US 33 extends 248 mi from the Ohio River at Ravenswood to the Virginia state line atop Shenandoah Mountain west of Harrisonburg, Virginia.

USS Shenandoah | Four United States Navy ships, including one rigid airship, and one ship of the Confederate States of America, have been named Shenandoah, after the Shenandoah River of western Virginia and West Virginia.

Long Marsh Run | Long Marsh Run is a small stream located in the Shenandoah Valley along the border of Virginia and West Virginia.  A tributary of the Shenandoah River, Long Marsh Run's headwaters is about three miles northeast of Berryville, Virginia, where it flows east into West Virginia and thence into the Shenandoah.

German River | The German River is the principal tributary of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River, flowing for 14.7 mi in the U.S. state of Virginia.  The river originates in northern Rockingham County, just east of the West Virginia border, in the George Washington National Forest, near the crest of Shenandoah Mountain in the Allegheny Mountains.
Bloomery, Jefferson County, West Virginia | Bloomery is an unincorporated community on the Shenandoah River in Jefferson County, West Virginia, United States.
response.summary_2: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River, and the Shenandoah River passes through the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.
